title.zh: 卷六十一 張騫李廣利傳
title.en: Volume 61: Zhang Qian and Li Guangli
title.raw: 卷六十一 張騫李廣利傳 Volume 61: Zhang Qian and Li Guangli

sentences.zh: 卷六十一 張騫李廣利傳第三十一

translations.literal: Volume 61: Biographies the Thirty-first, Zhang Qian and Li Guangli.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 61: Biography 31—Zhang Qian and Li Guangli.

sentences.zh: 張騫，漢中人也，建元中為郎。

translations.literal: Zhang Qian was a man of Hanzhong; during the Jianyuan era he served as a gentleman-at-court.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian came from Hanzhong and held a courtier's post in the Jianyuan years.

sentences.zh: 時，匈奴降者言匈奴破月氏王，以其頭為飲器，月氏遁而怨匈奴，無與共擊之。

translations.literal: At that time, defectors from the Xiongnu said the Xiongnu had defeated the king of the Yuezhi and made his skull into a drinking vessel; the Yuezhi had fled and resented the Xiongnu, but had no one to join them in striking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Defectors from the Xiongnu reported that the nomads had crushed the Yuezhi king and turned his skull into a cup; the Yuezhi had withdrawn in bitterness, with no ally willing to help them strike back.

sentences.zh: 漢方欲事滅胡，聞此言，欲通使，道必更匈奴中，乃募能使者。

translations.literal: Han was at that time intent on destroying the Hu; hearing these words, it wished to send envoys, but the route had necessarily to pass through the Xiongnu, so it recruited one who could go.
translations.idiomatic: The court was bent on breaking the northern tribes; on hearing this, it wanted to open contact with the Yuezhi, yet any road there ran through Xiongnu territory, so it called for a man willing to make the journey.

sentences.zh: 騫以郎應募，使月氏，與堂邑氏奴甘父俱出隴西。

translations.literal: Qian, as a gentleman-at-court, answered the recruitment and was sent to the Yuezhi, together with the Tangyi clan's slave Ganfu, both departing from Longxi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian, a courtier, volunteered for the mission to the Yuezhi and set out from Longxi with his guide Ganfu, a Tangyi household slave.

sentences.zh: 徑匈奴，匈奴得之，傳詣單于。

translations.literal: They went straight through the Xiongnu; the Xiongnu seized them and conveyed them to the chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: They cut across Xiongnu lands, were taken prisoner, and were escorted to the chanyu.

sentences.zh: 單于曰：「月氏在吾北，漢何以得往使？

translations.literal: The chanyu said, "The Yuezhi are to my north; how can Han go and send envoys there?
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu asked, "The Yuezhi lie north of me—how does Han imagine it can send envoys past us?

sentences.zh: 吾欲使越，漢肯聽我乎？」

translations.literal: If I wished to send envoys to Yue, would Han listen to me?"
translations.idiomatic: If I wanted an embassy to cross Han territory into Yue, would your court allow it?"

sentences.zh: 留騫十餘歲，予妻，有子，然騫持漢節不失。

translations.literal: He detained Qian for more than ten years, gave him a wife, and he had a son, yet Qian held fast to the Han credentials and did not lose them.
translations.idiomatic: Qian was held over ten years, given a wife and a child, but he never let go of his Han staff of office.

sentences.zh: 居匈奴西，騫因與其屬亡鄉月氏，西走數十日，至大宛。

translations.literal: Dwelling west of the Xiongnu, Qian thereupon with his followers fled, aiming for the Yuezhi; going west for several tens of days, they reached Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: Stationed west of the nomads, he slipped away with his party toward the Yuezhi; after many days' march westward they reached Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 大宛聞漢之饒財，欲通不得，見騫，喜，問欲何之。

translations.literal: Dayuan had heard that Han was rich in goods and wished to open communication but could not; seeing Qian, it rejoiced and asked where he wished to go.
translations.idiomatic: Dayuan had long heard of Han's wealth and wanted trade but could not reach it; delighted to see Qian, the king asked his destination.

sentences.zh: 騫曰：「為漢使月氏而為匈奴所閉道，今亡，唯王使人道送我。

translations.literal: Qian said, "I was sent by Han to the Yuezhi but the Xiongnu blocked the road; now I have fled; I only ask that the king send men to guide and escort me.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian said, "Han sent me to the Yuezhi; the Xiongnu cut the route. I have escaped—only ask that you provide guides to see me through."

sentences.zh: 誠得至，反漢，漢之賂遺王財物不可勝言。」

translations.literal: If I truly reach there and return to Han, Han's gifts and presents to the king in goods cannot be told in full."
translations.idiomatic: If I reach them and return, the gifts Han will send your majesty will be beyond reckoning."

sentences.zh: 大宛以為然，遣騫，為發道譯，抵康居。

translations.literal: Dayuan deemed it right, sent Qian off, and for him issued guides and interpreters until they reached Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: The king agreed, furnished interpreters and an escort, and sent him on to Kangju.

sentences.zh: 康居傳致大月氏。

translations.literal: Kangju passed him on until he reached the Greater Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: From Kangju he was relayed onward to the Greater Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 大月氏王已為胡所殺，立其夫人為王。

translations.literal: The king of the Greater Yuezhi had already been killed by the Hu, and his consort was established as ruler.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuezhi king had fallen to the nomads; his queen now ruled in his place.

sentences.zh: 既臣大夏而君之，地肥饒，少寇，志安樂。

translations.literal: Having already made Daxia subject and ruled it, the land was fertile and rich, bandits were few, and their intent was peace and pleasure.
translations.idiomatic: They had subdued Daxia and held its rich soil with little raiding; they were settled and had no wish for war.

sentences.zh: 又自以遠遠漢，殊無報胡之心。

translations.literal: Moreover they themselves considered themselves far, far from Han and altogether had no mind to repay the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: They also reckoned themselves impossibly distant from Han and felt no urge to avenge themselves on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 騫從月氏至大夏，竟不能得月氏要領。

translations.literal: Qian followed the Yuezhi to Daxia and in the end could not obtain the Yuezhi's main point.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian went on with them into Daxia but never secured a firm commitment from the Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 留歲餘，還，並南山，欲從羌中歸，復為匈奴所得。

translations.literal: He stayed over a year, then returned, keeping south of the mountains, intending to return through the Qiang region, and again was taken by the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: After more than a year he turned homeward along the southern ranges, hoping to slip through Qiang country, only to be seized again by the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 留歲餘，單于死，國內亂，騫與胡妻及堂邑父俱亡歸漢。

translations.literal: He was detained over a year; the chanyu died and the state was in turmoil within; Qian together with his Hu wife and Tangyi Fu all fled and returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Another year passed; the chanyu died and the realm fell into strife, and Zhang Qian fled to Han with his Xiongnu wife and Ganfu.

sentences.zh: 拜騫太中大夫，堂邑父為奉使君。

translations.literal: Qian was appointed grand counsellor of the palace; Tangyi Fu was made Fengshi lord.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor appointed Zhang Qian grand counsellor of the palace and ennobled Ganfu as Lord Fengshi for his part in the embassy.

sentences.zh: 騫為人強力，寬大信人，蠻夷愛之。

translations.literal: Qian as a person was forceful, broad-minded, and trusted others; the barbarians loved him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was resolute, generous, and trustworthy; frontier peoples warmed to him.

sentences.zh: 堂邑父胡人，善射，窮急射禽獸給食。

translations.literal: Tangyi Fu was a Hu man, skilled at archery; in extremity he shot birds and beasts to supply food.
translations.idiomatic: Ganfu, a northerner, was a fine bowman who, when supplies ran out, brought down game to keep them fed.

sentences.zh: 初，騫行時百餘人，去十三歲，唯二人得還。

translations.literal: At first, when Qian set out there were over a hundred persons; he was gone thirteen years; only two persons were able to return.
translations.idiomatic: He had left with more than a hundred men; thirteen years later only two came back.

sentences.zh: 騫身所至者，大宛、大月氏、大夏、康居，而傳聞其旁大國五六，具為天子言其地形所有，語皆在《西域傳》。

translations.literal: As for places Qian himself reached, there were Dayuan, the Greater Yuezhi, Daxia, and Kangju, and by hearsay five or six great states beside them; he fully reported to the Son of Heaven their terrain and what they possessed; the words are all in the "Treatise on the Western Regions."
translations.idiomatic: In person he had reached Dayuan, the Greater Yuezhi, Daxia, and Kangju, and by report knew five or six other great kingdoms nearby; he laid all of this—lands and resources—before the emperor, as recorded in the Western Regions treatise.

sentences.zh: 騫曰：「臣在大夏時，見邛竹杖、蜀布，問：『安得此？』

translations.literal: Qian said, "When your servant was in Daxia, I saw Qiong bamboo staffs and Shu cloth and asked, 'How did these come here?'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian said, "In Daxia I saw bamboo canes from Qiong and cloth from Shu and asked how such things had got there."

sentences.zh: 大夏國人曰：『吾賈人往市之身毒國。

translations.literal: The people of Daxia said, 'Our merchants went to buy them in the Shendu state.'
translations.idiomatic: They answered, 'Our traders brought them back from Shendu (India).'

sentences.zh: 身毒國在大夏東南可數千里。

translations.literal: The Shendu state is in Daxia's southeast, perhaps several thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: Shendu lies some thousands of li southeast of Daxia.

sentences.zh: 其俗土著，與大夏同，而卑濕暑熱。

translations.literal: Its customs are sedentary, the same as Daxia's, but low-lying, damp, and hot in summer.
translations.idiomatic: The people are settled like those of Daxia, but the land is low, humid, and sweltering.

sentences.zh: 其民乘象以戰。

translations.literal: Its people mount elephants to fight.
translations.idiomatic: They fight from the backs of elephants.

sentences.zh: 其國臨大水焉。』

translations.literal: Its state borders a great water.'"
translations.idiomatic: Their country fronts on a great river.'"

sentences.zh: 以騫度之，大夏去漢萬二千里，居西南。

translations.literal: By Qian's reckoning, Daxia is twelve thousand li from Han and lies in the southwest.
translations.idiomatic: By Zhang Qian's estimate Daxia stood twelve thousand li from the capital, to the southwest.

sentences.zh: 今身毒又居大夏東南數千里，有蜀物，此其去蜀不遠矣。

translations.literal: Now Shendu again lies several thousand li southeast of Daxia and has Shu goods; this means it is not far from Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Shendu lies thousands of li beyond Daxia to the southeast yet already has Shu goods—so it cannot be far from Shu itself.

sentences.zh: 今使大夏，從羌中，險，羌人惡之；

translations.literal: Now to send envoys to Daxia through the Qiang region is perilous, and the Qiang detest it;
translations.idiomatic: The route to Daxia through Qiang territory is dangerous, and the Qiang resent intruders;

sentences.zh: 少北，則為匈奴所得；

translations.literal: a little to the north, then one is taken by the Xiongnu;
translations.idiomatic: veer slightly north and the Xiongnu take you;

sentences.zh: 從蜀，宜徑，又無寇。」

translations.literal: from Shu it should be a direct route and moreover without raiders."
translations.idiomatic: from Shu the way should be straighter and free of marauders."

sentences.zh: 天子既聞大宛及大夏、安息之屬皆大國，多奇物，土著，頗與中國同俗，而兵弱，貴漢財物；

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven having heard that Dayuan and Daxia, Anxi, and the like were all great states, with many strange goods, sedentary populations, customs rather like China's, yet weak in arms and valuing Han goods;
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had learned that Dayuan, Daxia, Parthia, and their neighbors were large, settled kingdoms rich in curiosities, with customs not unlike China's, weak armies, and a hunger for Han wealth;

sentences.zh: 其北則大月氏、康居之屬，兵強，可以賂遺設利朝也。

translations.literal: to their north were the Greater Yuezhi, Kangju, and the like, strong in arms, who could be drawn by gifts and profit to court audience.
translations.idiomatic: north of them lay the Greater Yuezhi and Kangju—hard fighters who might nonetheless be courted with bribes and profit.

sentences.zh: 誠得而以義屬之，則廣地萬里，重九譯，致殊俗，威德遍於四海。

translations.literal: If truly obtained and by righteousness made subject, then lands would extend ten thousand li, repeated ninefold interpretation would reach strange customs, and awe-inspiring virtue would spread over the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: If they could be bound in good faith to the throne, Han might stretch ten thousand li, speak through chains of interpreters to distant peoples, and spread its majesty to the ends of the earth.

sentences.zh: 天子欣欣以騫言為然。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven gladly deemed Qian's words correct.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was delighted and accepted Zhang Qian's counsel.

sentences.zh: 乃令因蜀犍為發間使，四道並出：出駹，出莋，出徙、邛，出僰，皆各行一二千里。

translations.literal: Thereupon he ordered that from Shu and Jianwei secret envoys be dispatched, four routes going out together: out by Mang, out by Zuo, out by Xi and Qiong, out by Bo, each going one or two thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered secret missions from Shu and Jianwei along four routes—through Mang, Zuo, Xi and Qiong, and Bo—each probing one or two thousand li.

sentences.zh: 其北方閉氐、莋，南方閉巂、昆明。

translations.literal: On the northern side they were blocked by the Di and Zuo; on the southern side blocked by Xi and Kunming.
translations.idiomatic: The Di and Zuo blocked the northern parties; the Xi and Kunming barred the southern ones.

sentences.zh: 昆明之屬無君長，善寇盜，輒殺略漢使，終莫得通。

translations.literal: The Kunming sort had no rulers or chiefs, were good at raiding and plundering, repeatedly killed and robbed Han envoys, and in the end passage could not be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunming peoples had no chiefs, lived by raiding, killed and plundered Han envoys, and no road was ever opened through them.

sentences.zh: 然聞其西可千餘里，有乘象國，名滇越，而蜀賈間出物者或至焉，於是漢以求大復道始通滇國。

translations.literal: Yet it was heard that west of them perhaps over a thousand li there was an elephant-riding state named Dianyue, and Shu merchants who occasionally went out with goods sometimes reached there; thereupon Han, in seeking the road to Daxia, first opened communication with the Dian state.
translations.idiomatic: Word had it that a thousand li west lay an elephant-riding realm called Dianyue, where Shu merchants sometimes appeared; in pursuit of a road to Daxia, Han thus opened relations with Dian for the first time.

sentences.zh: 初，漢欲通西南夷，費多，罷之。

translations.literal: At first, Han wished to open communication with the southwestern Yi, but the expense was great and it abandoned this.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier attempts to reach the southwestern tribes had been dropped as too costly.

sentences.zh: 及騫言可以通大夏，及復事西南夷。

translations.literal: When Qian said one could reach Daxia, then again they took up affairs with the southwestern Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian's report that Daxia could be reached from the southwest revived the whole frontier policy.

sentences.zh: 騫以校尉從大將軍擊匈奴，知水草處，軍得以不乏，乃封騫為博望侯。

translations.literal: Qian as colonel followed the grand general in striking the Xiongnu; he knew the places of water and grass, so the army was able not to lack supplies, and thereupon Qian was enfeoffed as marquis of Bowang.
translations.idiomatic: As a colonel under the grand general he guided the army to water and pasture so the troops did not want; for this he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Bowang.

sentences.zh: 是歲，元朔六年也。

translations.literal: That year was the sixth year of Yuanshuo.
translations.idiomatic: This was the sixth year of Yuanshuo (123 BCE).

sentences.zh: 後二年，騫為衛尉，與李廣俱出右北平擊匈奴。

translations.literal: After two years, Qian was commandant of the guards and together with Li Guang both went out of Youbeiping to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Two years later, as commandant of the guards, he joined Li Guang in a sortie from Youbeiping against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 匈奴圍李將軍，軍失亡多，而騫後期當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu surrounded General Li; the army lost and fled many, while Qian was late to the rendezvous and deserved execution; he ransomed himself and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu encircled Li Guang's command with heavy losses; Zhang Qian arrived late, faced capital sentence, ransomed his rank away, and was reduced to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 是歲，驃騎將軍破匈奴西邊，殺數萬人，至祁連山。

translations.literal: That year the flying-charge general broke the Xiongnu western border, killed several tens of thousands, and reached the Qilian Mountains.
translations.idiomatic: That same year the flying-charge general shattered the western Xiongnu, killing tens of thousands and pushing to the Qilian range.

sentences.zh: 其秋，渾邪王率眾降漢，而金城、河西並南山至鹽澤，空無匈奴。

translations.literal: That autumn the king of Hunye led his masses to surrender to Han, and from Jincheng and Hexi along the southern mountains to the Salt Marsh it was empty of Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn Hunye king came over with his people, and from Jincheng and the Hexi corridor south to the salt lakes the steppe lay empty of nomads.

sentences.zh: 匈奴時有候者到，而希矣。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu at times had scouts arrive, but they were rare.
translations.idiomatic: Occasional Xiongnu scouts still appeared, but they were few.

sentences.zh: 後二年，漢擊走單于於幕北。

translations.literal: After two years, Han struck and drove the chanyu north of the desert curtain.
translations.idiomatic: Two years later Han drove the chanyu beyond the northern desert.

sentences.zh: 天子數問騫大夏之屬。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven repeatedly asked Qian about Daxia and the like.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor often pressed Zhang Qian for news of Daxia and the western states.

sentences.zh: 騫既失侯，因曰：「臣居匈奴中，聞烏孫王號昆莫。

translations.literal: Qian having lost his marquisate, thereupon said, "When your servant dwelt among the Xiongnu, I heard the Wusun king is titled Kunmo.
translations.idiomatic: Stripped of his fief, Zhang Qian said, "Among the Xiongnu I heard that the Wusun ruler bears the title Kunmo."

sentences.zh: 昆莫父難兜靡本與大月氏俱在祁連、敦煌間，小國也。

translations.literal: Kunmo's father Nandoumi originally together with the Greater Yuezhi were between Qilian and Dunhuang, a small state.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo's father Nandoumi had ruled a small people between the Qilian range and Dunhuang alongside the Greater Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 大月氏攻殺難兜靡，奪其地，人民亡走匈奴。

translations.literal: The Greater Yuezhi attacked and killed Nandoumi, seized his land, and the people fled in disorder to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Yuezhi slew Nandoumi, seized his territory, and his followers fled to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 子昆莫新生，傅父布就翕侯抱亡置草中，為求食，還，見狼乳之，又烏銜肉翔其旁，以為神，遂持歸匈奴，單于愛養之。

translations.literal: The son Kunmo was newly born; the foster father Bu-jiu Xihou took him in flight, placed him in the grass, went to seek food, returned, saw a wolf suckling him, and moreover a crow holding meat circling beside him; deeming him divine, he then carried him back to the Xiongnu, and the chanyu cherished and raised him.
translations.idiomatic: The infant Kunmo was carried off by his nurse-father Bu-jiu the Xihou, hidden in the grass while food was sought; returning, the man found a wolf nursing the child and crows dropping meat nearby. Convinced the boy was spirit-touched, he brought him to the chanyu, who reared him with favor.

sentences.zh: 及壯，以其父民眾與昆莫，使將兵，數有功。

translations.literal: When he was grown, the chanyu gave him his father's people and masses, ordered him to lead troops, and several times he had merit.
translations.idiomatic: When the boy came of age, the chanyu restored his father's tribesmen to him, gave him a command, and he won repeated distinction.

sentences.zh: 時，月氏已為匈奴所破，西擊塞王。

translations.literal: At that time the Yuezhi had already been broken by the Xiongnu and went west to strike the Sai king.
translations.idiomatic: By then the Xiongnu had shattered the Yuezhi, who marched west against the Sai king.

sentences.zh: 塞王南走遠徙，月氏居其地。

translations.literal: The Sai king fled south and removed far; the Yuezhi dwelt in his land.
translations.idiomatic: The Sai king withdrew far to the south, and the Yuezhi took his ground.

sentences.zh: 昆莫既健，自請單于報父怨，遂西攻破大月氏。

translations.literal: Kunmo having become strong, himself requested of the chanyu to repay his father's grudge, and thereupon went west and broke the Greater Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: Once Kunmo grew strong he begged leave to avenge his father; marching west, he crushed the Greater Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 大月氏復西走，徒大夏地。

translations.literal: The Greater Yuezhi again fled west and moved to Daxia's territory.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuezhi fled farther west and settled on Daxia's soil.

sentences.zh: 昆莫略其眾，因留居，兵稍強，會單于死，不肯復朝事匈奴。

translations.literal: Kunmo plundered their masses, thereupon stayed and dwelt, arms gradually grew strong, it happened the chanyu died, and he was unwilling again to attend court and serve the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo absorbed their warriors, stayed put, and grew in strength; when the chanyu died he refused ever again to acknowledge Xiongnu overlordship.

sentences.zh: 匈奴遣兵擊之，不勝，益以為神而遠之。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu sent troops to strike him, did not prevail, and increasingly deemed him divine and kept distant from him.
translations.idiomatic: Xiongnu attacks failed; they came to regard him as uncanny and gave him a wide berth.

sentences.zh: 今單于新困於漢，而昆莫地空。

translations.literal: Now the chanyu has newly been distressed by Han, while Kunmo's land is empty.
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu is newly worsted by Han, and Kunmo's old pasture lands lie open.

sentences.zh: 蠻夷戀故地，又貪漢物，誠以此時厚賂烏孫，招以東居故地，漢遣公主為夫人，結昆弟，其勢宜聽，則是斷匈奴右臂也。

translations.literal: The barbarian Yi cling to old lands and moreover covet Han goods; if truly at this time one richly bribes Wusun, summons them to dwell east in the old lands, Han sends a princess as consort, binds brotherhood, by the situation they ought to listen—then this is cutting off the Xiongnu's right arm.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier peoples cling to ancestral ground yet hunger for Han goods; rich gifts now could draw the Wusun back east to their old pastures, a Han princess sealing a brotherly pact—they would surely listen, and you would sever the Xiongnu's right arm.

sentences.zh: 既連烏孫，自其西大夏之屬皆可招來而為外臣。」

translations.literal: Having already linked Wusun, from west of them Daxia and the like can all be summoned and come to be outer subjects."
translations.idiomatic: Once Wusun is bound to us, Daxia and the states beyond can be summoned as outer vassals."

sentences.zh: 天子以為然，拜騫為中郎將，將三百人，馬各二匹，牛、羊以萬數，繼金幣帛直數千巨萬，多持節副使，道可便遣之旁國。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven deemed it right, appointed Qian general of the gentlemen of the household, leading three hundred men, each with two horses, cattle and sheep in tens of thousands, carrying gold, coins, and silks worth several thousand times ten thousand, many holding credentials as deputy envoys, on the road being able as convenient to dispatch them to neighboring states.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed, named Zhang Qian general of the household with three hundred followers, two mounts each, vast herds of stock, and treasure worth tens of millions in cash and silk, with many credentialled deputies who could be sent on to neighboring courts as the route allowed.

sentences.zh: 騫既至烏孫，致賜諭指，未能得其決。

translations.literal: Qian having reached Wusun, conveyed gifts and explained intent, was not able to obtain their decision.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian reached Wusun with gifts and the emperor's message but could not secure a firm answer.

sentences.zh: 語在《西域傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the "Treatise on the Western Regions."
translations.idiomatic: The details are given in the Western Regions treatise.

sentences.zh: 騫即分遣副使使大宛、康居、月氏、大夏。

translations.literal: Qian thereupon separately dispatched deputy envoys to go to Dayuan, Kangju, the Yuezhi, and Daxia.
translations.idiomatic: He at once sent deputies on to Dayuan, Kangju, the Yuezhi, and Daxia.

sentences.zh: 烏孫發道譯送騫，與烏孫使數十人，馬數十匹。

translations.literal: Wusun issued guides and interpreters to escort Qian, together with several tens of Wusun envoys and several tens of horses.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun furnished guides to escort him home with dozens of their envoys and mounts.

sentences.zh: 報謝，因令窺漢，知其廣大。

translations.literal: They returned thanks; thereupon Han had them observe Han and know its vastness.
translations.idiomatic: They came to offer thanks, and the court had them tour the realm until they understood how vast Han was.

sentences.zh: 騫還，拜為大行。

translations.literal: Qian returned and was appointed grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: On his return Zhang Qian was appointed grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，騫卒。

translations.literal: After more than a year, Qian died.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later he died.

sentences.zh: 後歲餘，其所遣副使通大夏之屬者皆頗與其人俱來，於是西北國始通於漢矣。

translations.literal: After more than a year, those deputy envoys he had dispatched who had opened communication with Daxia and the like all largely came together with their people; thereupon the northwestern states first opened communication with Han.
translations.idiomatic: Within another year the deputies he had sent toward Daxia began returning with foreign retinues, and the northwestern kingdoms were at last in steady contact with Han.

sentences.zh: 然騫鑿空，諸後使往者皆稱博望侯，以為質於外國，外國由是信之。

translations.literal: Yet Qian bored the void; all later envoys who went all called themselves marquis of Bowang, using this as pledge in foreign states, and foreign states thereby trusted them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian had opened the blank on the map; later envoys all called themselves the Marquis of Bowang to win trust abroad, and the western courts believed them for it.

sentences.zh: 其後，烏孫竟與漢結婚。

translations.literal: After that, Wusun in the end allied in marriage with Han.
translations.idiomatic: In time the Wusun did take a Han princess in marriage.

sentences.zh: 初，天子發書《易》，曰「神馬當從西北來」。

translations.literal: At first, the Son of Heaven opened the document Changes, and it said, "The divine horse shall come from the northwest."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had once consulted the Changes and read the omen: "A spirit horse will come from the northwest."

sentences.zh: 得烏孫馬好，名曰：「天馬」。

translations.literal: Obtaining Wusun horses that were fine, they named them "heavenly horses."
translations.idiomatic: The fine Wusun mounts were dubbed "heavenly horses."

sentences.zh: 及得宛汗血馬，益壯，更名烏孫馬曰「西極馬」，宛馬曰「天馬」云。

translations.literal: When they obtained Ferghana blood-sweating horses, still more robust, they changed the name of Wusun horses to "western-pole horses" and Ferghana horses to "heavenly horses," it is said.
translations.idiomatic: When the blood-sweating Ferghana horses arrived—still hardier—the Wusun stock was renamed "horses of the western pole" and the Ferghana animals alone kept the title "heavenly horses."

sentences.zh: 而漢始築令居以西，初置酒泉郡，以通西北國。

translations.literal: And Han for the first time built west of Lingju, first established Jiuquan commandery, in order to open communication with the northwestern states.
translations.idiomatic: Han began building the wall west of Lingju and founded Jiuquan commandery to hold the road open to the northwest.

sentences.zh: 因《益》發使抵安息、奄蔡、□、條支、身毒國。

translations.literal: On the basis of the Changes (hexagram Yi), envoys were dispatched reaching Anxi, Yancai, a state whose name is missing in the text (□), Tiaozhi, and Shendu.
translations.idiomatic: Taking their cue from the Changes, the court sent missions to Parthia, Yancai, a kingdom the manuscript leaves blank, Characene on the Persian Gulf, and Shendu (India).

sentences.zh: 而天子好宛馬，使者相望於道，一輩大者數百，少者百餘人，所繼操，大放博望侯時。

translations.literal: And the Son of Heaven loved Ferghana horses; envoys thronged the roads; a large party numbered several hundred, a small one still over a hundred; the goods and equipage they carried in train far exceeded what had been sent in the Marquis of Bowang's day.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's passion for Ferghana horses filled the highways with embassies—some missions ran to hundreds of men, none to fewer than a hundred—with baggage trains dwarfing Zhang Qian's first expedition.

sentences.zh: 其後益習而衰少焉。

translations.literal: After that they increasingly became practiced and declined and diminished.
translations.idiomatic: Later, as the routes grew familiar, the traffic thinned.

sentences.zh: 漢率一歲中使者多者十餘，少者五六輩，遠者八九歲，近者數歲而反。

translations.literal: Han as a rule in one year had envoys in the many case over ten, in the few case five or six parties; those far took eight or nine years, those near several years and returned.
translations.idiomatic: In a typical year Han sent ten or more missions, or as few as five or six; the long journeys took eight or nine years, shorter ones several years, before men returned.

sentences.zh: 是時，漢既滅越，蜀所通西南夷皆震，請吏。

translations.literal: At this time, Han having already destroyed Yue, the southwestern Yi communicated with by Shu all trembled and requested officials.
translations.idiomatic: Han had just crushed Nanyue; the southwestern tribes reached from Shu were awed and begged to be placed under Han magistrates.

sentences.zh: 置牂柯、越巂、益州、沈黎、文山郡，欲地接以前通大夏。

translations.literal: They established Zangke, Yuexi, Yizhou, Shenli, and Wenshan commanderies, desiring the lands to connect so as previously to open communication with Daxia.
translations.idiomatic: The court set up Zangke, Yuexi, Yizhou, Shenli, and Wenshan commanderies, pushing frontiers forward to link with the road toward Daxia.

sentences.zh: 乃遣使歲十餘輩，出此初郡，皆復閉昆明，為所殺，奪幣物。

translations.literal: Thereupon they dispatched envoys year after year over ten parties, going out from these newly established commanderies, all again being blocked by Kunming, by them being killed, and gifts and goods seized.
translations.idiomatic: Year after year a dozen or more missions left these new commands, only to be stopped by the Kunming peoples, who killed the envoys and seized their goods.

sentences.zh: 於是漢發兵擊昆明，斬首數萬。

translations.literal: Thereupon Han raised troops to strike Kunming and beheaded several tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Han then sent an army against Kunming and claimed tens of thousands of heads.

sentences.zh: 後復遣使，竟不得通。

translations.literal: After again dispatching envoys, in the end passage could not be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Further embassies still failed to open the road.

sentences.zh: 語在《西南夷傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the "Treatise on the Southwestern Yi."
translations.idiomatic: The story is told in the Southwestern Yi treatise.

sentences.zh: 自騫開外國道以尊貴，其吏士爭上書言外國奇怪利害，求使。

translations.literal: From the time Qian opened the road to foreign states and became honored, his clerks and soldiers vied in submitting memorials speaking of foreign states' strangenesses, wonders, profits, and harms, seeking to be sent as envoys.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Qian's opening of the western routes brought him rank and favor, officials and troops vied to memorialize about exotic lands, gains, and risks—all begging for an embassy.

sentences.zh: 天子為其絕遠，非人所樂，聽其言，予節，募吏民無問所從來，為具備人眾遣之，以廣其道。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven because it was utterly distant, not what men delight in, listened to their words, gave them credentials, recruited clerks and commoners without asking whence they came, for them fully prepared men and masses and dispatched them, in order to broaden the road.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor knew the journey was grim and uninviting, yet he heard them out, issued staffs of office, and recruited clerks and commoners of any origin, fitting out full parties to widen the traffic in every direction.

sentences.zh: 來還不能無侵盜幣物，及使失指，天子為其習之，輒復按致重罪，以激怒令贖，復求使。

translations.literal: Coming back they could not but sometimes encroach on and steal goods and silks, and as for envoys missing the intent, the Son of Heaven because they were practiced in this, repeatedly again investigated and brought heavy crimes, in order to provoke anger and order ransom, again seeking to be envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Returning missions could not help embezzling part of the imperial gifts, or bungling their instructions; the emperor, used to the pattern, would indict them on grave charges, let them buy their way out, and watch them volunteer for another posting.

sentences.zh: 使端無窮，而輕犯法。

translations.literal: The pretexts for missions were endless, while they lightly violated the law.
translations.idiomatic: Excuses for new embassies multiplied, and men flouted the statutes with little fear.

sentences.zh: 其吏卒亦輒復盛推外國所有，言大者予節，言小者為副，故妄言無行之徒皆爭相效。

translations.literal: Their clerks and soldiers also repeatedly again greatly exaggerated what foreign states possessed; those whose words were large were given credentials, those whose words were small became deputies; therefore those who falsely spoke and were without conduct all vied to imitate one another.
translations.idiomatic: Subordinates too puffed up the riches of the west: the grander the tale, the likelier the staff of office; smaller boasts won deputy posts—so liars and riffraff rushed to copy one another.

sentences.zh: 其使皆私縣官繼物，欲賤市以私其利。

translations.literal: Their envoys all privately embezzled the state's goods they carried in train, wishing to buy cheap and privatize the profit.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys skimmed the imperial gifts they escorted, planning to buy cheap on the side and pocket the difference.

sentences.zh: 外國亦厭漢使人人有言輕重，度漢兵遠，不能至，而禁其食物，以苦漢使。

translations.literal: The foreign states also wearied of Han envoys, each man having words of weight or lightness; they reckoned Han troops were far and could not arrive, and cut off their food, thereby distressing Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Western courts tired of Han's endless envoys, judged the armies too distant to help, and starved the missions to break them.

sentences.zh: 漢使乏絕，責怨，至相攻擊。

translations.literal: Han envoys were exhausted and cut off, blamed and resented one another, and reached the point of attacking one another.
translations.idiomatic: Stranded missions turned on one another, blaming and even fighting among themselves.

sentences.zh: 樓蘭、姑師小國，當空道，攻劫漢使王恢等尤甚。

translations.literal: Loulan and Jushi, small states, stood athwart the open road and attacked and robbed Han envoys such as Wang Hui especially severely.
translations.idiomatic: The petty kingdoms of Loulan and Jushi straddled the caravan road and preyed worst of all on missions such as Wang Hui's.

sentences.zh: 而匈奴奇兵又時時遮擊之。

translations.literal: Moreover Xiongnu irregular troops also from time to time intercepted and struck them.
translations.idiomatic: Xiongnu raiding parties joined in, picking off stragglers at will.

sentences.zh: 使者爭言外國利害，皆有城邑，兵弱易擊。

translations.literal: The envoys vied in speaking of foreign states' profits and harms, that all had walled towns, weak troops, and were easy to strike.
translations.idiomatic: Returning envoys competed to insist that every western state had cities, feeble armies, and was ripe for the taking.

sentences.zh: 於是天子遣從票侯破奴將屬國騎及郡兵數萬以擊胡，胡皆去。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven sent Marquis Congpiao Punu leading dependent-state cavalry and commandery troops numbering several tens of thousands to strike the Hu, and the Hu all departed.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent Marquis Congpiao Punu at the head of tens of thousands of allied horsemen and local levies; the nomads melted away.

sentences.zh: 明年，擊破姑師，虜樓蘭王。

translations.literal: The next year they smashed Jushi and captured the king of Loulan.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Jushi was crushed and the king of Loulan taken prisoner.

sentences.zh: 酒泉列亭障至玉門矣。

translations.literal: From Jiuquan watchtowers and barriers were laid in a line to Jade Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Signal towers and walls now ran from Jiuquan clear to the Jade Gate.

sentences.zh: 而大宛諸國發使隨漢使來，觀漢廣大，以大鳥卵及□眩人獻於漢，天子大悅。

translations.literal: And Dayuan and the various states sent envoys following Han envoys to come, observing Han's vastness, and with large birds' eggs and illusionists (text defective: □) presented them to Han; the Son of Heaven was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Dayuan and its neighbors sent retinues back with Han missions to see the empire for themselves, bringing ostrich eggs and conjurers (one character missing in the manuscript); the emperor was delighted.

sentences.zh: 而漢使窮河源，其山多玉石，採來，天子案古圖書，名河所出山曰崑崙雲。

translations.literal: And Han envoys traced the river's source to its end; that mountain had much jade and stone; they gathered and brought them; the Son of Heaven examined ancient charts and documents and named the mountain whence the river issues Kunlun, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Han scouts tracked the Yellow River to its source, where jade-strewn peaks yielded specimens for the throne; comparing old maps, the court declared those heights Kunlun.

sentences.zh: 是時，上方數巡狩海上，乃悉從外國客，大都多人則過之，散財帛賞賜，厚具饒給之，以覽視漢富厚焉。

translations.literal: At this time the sovereign was repeatedly hunting and inspecting along the sea; thereupon he entirely took along foreign guests; where a great metropolis had many people he passed through it, scattering silks and coins as rewards, richly preparing abundant supplies for them, in order to let them survey and see Han's wealth and thickness.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was touring the coast; he brought foreign guests in his train, detouring through populous cities, showering them with silk and cash and lavish rations so they could see how rich Han was.

sentences.zh: 大角氐，出奇戲諸怪物，多聚觀者，行賞賜，酒池肉林，令外國客遍觀名各倉庫府臧之積，欲以見漢廣大，傾駭之。

translations.literal: Great horned wrestlers, displaying strange plays and various monsters, many gathered watchers; he carried out rewards and gifts; pools of wine and forests of meat; he ordered foreign guests everywhere to view the accumulations in each named granary, treasury, and storehouse, wishing thereby to show Han's vastness and overturn and awe them.
translations.idiomatic: He staged bullfights, exotic spectacles, and freak shows before crowds, heaped rewards on guests, spread the proverbial pools of wine and forests of meat, and marched them through every state granary and treasury—anything to stagger them with Han's scale.

sentences.zh: 及加其眩者之工，而角氐奇戲歲增變，其益興，自此始。

translations.literal: And increasing the illusionists' craft, while horned-wrestler strange plays yearly increased and changed, their further flourishing began from this.
translations.idiomatic: Conjurers grew more skilled, the arena shows multiplied and changed year by year, and the fashion for such display dates from this period.

sentences.zh: 而外國使更來更去。

translations.literal: And foreign envoys came and went in alternation.
translations.idiomatic: Foreign embassies began passing in and out in a steady stream.

sentences.zh: 大宛以西皆自恃遠，尚驕恣，未可詘以禮羈縻而使也。

translations.literal: West of Dayuan all relied on distance, still were arrogant and self-willed, and could not be bent by ritual halter-and-bridle and sent as envoys.
translations.idiomatic: States west of Ferghana trusted their remoteness, stayed arrogant, and could not yet be managed with courtesy alone.

sentences.zh: 漢使往既多，其少從率進孰於天子，言大宛有善馬在貳師城，匿不肯示漢使。

translations.literal: Han envoys having gone in great number, their young followers mostly advanced what was ripe to the Son of Heaven, saying Dayuan had fine horses at Ershi city, hid them, and was unwilling to show Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: So many missions had returned that junior attendants routinely briefed the throne: Ferghana kept its best bloodstock at Ershi and refused to show Han's envoys.

sentences.zh: 天子既好宛馬，聞之甘心，使壯士車令等待千金及金馬以請宛王貳師城善馬。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven already loved Ferghana horses; hearing this he was willing at heart, and sent the stalwart Che Ling and others bearing a thousand in gold weight and a golden horse to request of the king of Dayuan the fine horses at Ershi city.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, already horse-mad, sent Che Ling with a fortune in gold and a golden horse to buy the Ershi studs from the king of Ferghana.

sentences.zh: 宛國饒漢物，相與謀曰：「漢去我遠，而鹽水中數有敗，出其北有胡寇，出其南乏水草，又且往往而絕邑，乏食者多。

translations.literal: The state of Dayuan was abundant in Han goods; they plotted together, saying, "Han is far from us, and in the salt water there are frequent disasters; going out northward there are Hu raiders, going out southward there is lack of water and grass, and moreover there are often cut-off settlements; many lack food.
translations.idiomatic: Ferghana was awash in Han luxuries. Its nobles reasoned: "The capital lies far beyond the salt desert, where caravans founder; north lie the nomads, south little water or grass, and long stretches without towns—most parties starve."

sentences.zh: 漢使數百人為輩來，常乏食，死者過半，是安能致大軍乎？

translations.literal: Han envoys come in parties of several hundred men and always lack food; the dead exceed half—how in this case can they bring a great army?
translations.idiomatic: Han missions arrive in hundreds, usually half dead of hunger—how could they field a real army?

sentences.zh: 且貳師馬，宛寶馬也。」

translations.literal: Moreover the Ershi horses are Dayuan's treasure horses."
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the Ershi horses are our kingdom's treasure."

sentences.zh: 遂不肯予漢使。

translations.literal: Thereupon they were unwilling to give them to Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: They refused the sale.

sentences.zh: 漢使怒，妄言，椎金馬而去。

translations.literal: The Han envoy was angry, spoke wildly, smashed the golden horse, and departed.
translations.idiomatic: The Han envoy lost his temper, hurled abuse, shattered the golden horse, and stalked off.

sentences.zh: 宛中貴人怒曰：「漢使至輕我！」

translations.literal: Dayuan's great men were angry and said, "The Han envoy has come to the utmost in belittling us!"
translations.idiomatic: Ferghana's grandees cried, "This envoy has insulted us beyond bearing!"

sentences.zh: 遣漢使去，令其東邊郁成王遮攻，殺漢使，取其財物。

translations.literal: They sent the Han envoy away, ordered the king of Yucheng on their eastern border to intercept and attack, killed the Han envoy, and took his goods.
translations.idiomatic: They sent the party on its way, then had Yucheng king on the eastern march ambush it, kill the envoys, and seize the treasure.

sentences.zh: 天子大怒。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven was greatly angry.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was furious.

sentences.zh: 諸嘗使宛姚定漢等言：「宛兵弱，誠以漢兵不過三千人，強弩射之，即破宛矣。」

translations.literal: Those who had formerly been envoys to Dayuan, Yao Dinghan and others, said, "Dayuan's troops are weak; truly if Han troops do not exceed three thousand men, with strong crossbows shooting them, then Dayuan will be broken at once."
translations.idiomatic: Veterans of Ferghana such as Yao Dinghan assured the court: "Their army is feeble—three thousand Han crossbowmen would shatter them."

sentences.zh: 天子以嘗使浞野侯攻樓蘭，以七百騎先至，虜其王，以定漢等言為然，而欲侯寵姬李氏，乃以李廣利為將軍，伐宛。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven because Zhuoye marquis had once attacked Loulan, with seven hundred cavalry arriving first and capturing its king, deemed Yao Dinghan and others' words correct, and wishing to enfeoff the favored concubine Li clan, thereupon made Li Guangli general and attacked Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor remembered how Zhuoye marquis had taken Loulan with seven hundred horsemen, believed the optimists, and wanting a fief for his favorite Lady Li's kin, named Li Guangli general and sent him against Ferghana.

sentences.zh: 孫 猛

translations.literal: Sun Meng.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Meng.

sentences.zh: 騫孫猛，字子游，有俊才，元帝時為光祿大夫，使匈奴，給事中，為石顯所譖。

translations.literal: Qian's grandson Meng, courtesy name Ziyou, had outstanding talent; in Yuandi's time he was grand counsellor of the palace, was sent to the Xiongnu, was attendant within the palace, and was slandered by Shi Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian's grandson Sun Meng, styled Ziyou, was a brilliant man who rose to grand counsellor under Emperor Yuan, served on the Xiongnu embassy staff, sat in the inner court, and fell to Shi Xian's slander.

sentences.zh: 自殺。

translations.literal: He killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: He took his own life.

sentences.zh: 李廣利

translations.literal: Li Guangli.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guangli.

sentences.zh: 李廣利，女弟李夫人有寵於上，產昌邑哀王。

translations.literal: Li Guangli—his younger sister Lady Li had favor with the sovereign and bore King Ai of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guangli's sister, the favored Lady Li, had borne Prince Ai of Changyi.

sentences.zh: 太初元年，以廣利為貳師將軍，發屬國六千騎及郡國惡少年數萬人以往，期至貳師城取善馬，故號「貳師將軍」。

translations.literal: In the first year of Taichu, Guangli was made Ershi general; they raised six thousand cavalry from dependent states and several tens of thousands of rowdy youths from commanderies and kingdoms to go, the term being to reach Ershi city and take fine horses; therefore he was titled "Ershi general."
translations.idiomatic: In Taichu 1 (104 BCE) he was named Ershi general and given six thousand tribal horse and tens of thousands of adventurers from the commanderies—his brief was to seize the bloodstock at Ershi, whence the title "General of Ershi."

sentences.zh: 故浩侯王恢使道軍。

translations.literal: Former Marquis of Hao Wang Hui was sent to guide the army.
translations.idiomatic: The former Marquis of Hao, Wang Hui, served as route guide for the expedition.

sentences.zh: 既西過鹽水，當道小國各堅城守，不肯給食，攻之不能下。

translations.literal: Having gone west and crossed the salt water, each small state athwart the road firmly guarded its walls, was unwilling to supply food, and when attacked could not be taken.
translations.idiomatic: West of the salt desert every petty state barred its gates, refused provisions, and could not be stormed.

sentences.zh: 下者得食，不下者數日則去。

translations.literal: Those taken supplied food; those not taken, after several days, then departed.
translations.idiomatic: Cities that yielded fed them; those that held out were abandoned after a few days.

sentences.zh: 比至郁成，士財有數千，皆饑罷。

translations.literal: By the time they reached Yucheng, soldiers were only several thousand in number, all hungry and exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: By Yucheng only a few thousand starving, spent men remained.

sentences.zh: 攻郁成城，郁成距之，所殺傷甚眾。

translations.literal: They attacked Yucheng city; Yucheng resisted them, and those killed and wounded were very many.
translations.idiomatic: They assaulted Yucheng; the garrison held, and Han losses were heavy.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍與左右計：「至郁成尚不能舉，況至其王都乎？」

translations.literal: The Ershi general consulted his staff: "Reaching Yucheng we still cannot raise it—how much more their royal capital?"
translations.idiomatic: General Ershi told his officers, "If we cannot crack Yucheng, what hope have we of the capital?"

sentences.zh: 引而還。

translations.literal: He led them and returned.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered a retreat.

sentences.zh: 往來二歲，至敦煌，士不過什一二。

translations.literal: Coming and going two years, reaching Dunhuang, soldiers were not more than one or two in ten.
translations.idiomatic: Two years out and back brought them to Dunhuang with barely one man in ten.

sentences.zh: 使使上書言：「道遠，多乏食，且士卒不患戰而患饑。

translations.literal: He sent envoys to submit a memorial saying, "The road is long, food is often lacking, and moreover the soldiers do not fear battle but fear hunger.
translations.idiomatic: He memorialized: "The march is long, rations fail, and the men dread hunger more than combat.

sentences.zh: 人少，不足以拔宛。

translations.literal: The men are few, insufficient to uproot Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: We are too few to take Ferghana.

sentences.zh: 願且罷兵，益發而復往。」

translations.literal: We beg for the moment to halt troops, increase levies, and go again."
translations.idiomatic: Pray let us stand down, raise fresh troops, and try again."

sentences.zh: 天子聞之，大怒，使使遮玉門關，曰：「軍有敢入，斬之。」

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven heard this and was greatly angry; he sent envoys to block Jade Gate pass, saying, "If any in the army dares enter, behead him."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor raged and posted guards at the Jade Gate: any soldier who crossed inward would die.

sentences.zh: 貳師恐，因留屯敦煌。

translations.literal: Ershi was afraid and thereupon remained garrisoned at Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi dared not advance and camped at Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 其夏，漢亡浞野之兵二萬餘於匈奴，公卿議者皆願罷宛軍，專力攻胡。

translations.literal: That summer Han lost Zhuoye's troops, over twenty thousand men, to the Xiongnu; those who deliberated among the dukes and ministers all wished to halt the Dayuan army and concentrate force on attacking the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: That summer Han lost twenty thousand men under Zhuoye marquis to the Xiongnu; ministers urged abandoning the Ferghana campaign to hit the nomads alone.

sentences.zh: 天子業出兵誅宛，宛小國而不能下，則大夏之屬漸輕漢，而宛善馬絕不來，烏孫、輪台易苦漢使，為外國笑。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven having already sent out troops to punish Dayuan—if a small state of Dayuan cannot be taken, then Daxia and the like will gradually despise Han, and Dayuan's fine horses will absolutely not come, Wusun and Luntai will easily distress Han envoys, and it will be laughed at by foreign states.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had already committed the host: if Han could not crush so small a kingdom, Daxia and the rest would lose respect, Ferghana's horses would never flow east, Wusun and Luntai would bully every envoy, and the western courts would mock the empire.

sentences.zh: 乃案言伐宛尤不便者鄧光等。

translations.literal: Thereupon he investigated and prosecuted Deng Guang and others who had said attacking Dayuan was especially inconvenient.
translations.idiomatic: He had men such as Deng Guang arrested for arguing most loudly against the invasion.

sentences.zh: 赦囚徒扞寇盜，發惡少年及邊騎，歲余而出敦煌六萬人，負私從者不與。

translations.literal: He pardoned convicts fit to repel raiders and bandits, raised rowdy youths and frontier cavalry, and after more than a year sixty thousand men marched out of Dunhuang, not counting those who followed on private account.
translations.idiomatic: Convicts fit for frontier duty, street toughs, and border horse were mobilized; after a year of preparation sixty thousand men crossed from Dunhuang, private followers not counted.

sentences.zh: 牛十萬，馬三萬匹，驢、橐駝以萬數繼糧，兵弩甚設。

translations.literal: Cattle one hundred thousand, horses thirty thousand head, donkeys and camels in myriads to carry grain in train, arms and crossbows very fully provided.
translations.idiomatic: The train included a hundred thousand oxen, thirty thousand horses, and tens of thousands of donkeys and camels for grain, with crossbows and weapons in full supply.

sentences.zh: 天下騷動，轉相奉伐宛，五十餘校尉。

translations.literal: All under heaven was stirred; relaying one another they supplied the attack on Dayuan—over fifty colonels.
translations.idiomatic: The realm seethed as relays of supply fed more than fifty colonels on the Ferghana expedition.

sentences.zh: 宛城中無井，汲城外流水，於是遣水工徙其城下水空以穴其城。

translations.literal: Dayuan's city had no wells within; it drew flowing water outside the walls; thereupon he sent water engineers to divert the water below the city hollow so as to tunnel the city.
translations.idiomatic: Ferghana's capital had no wells and drew water from a stream outside; Han sent engineers to divert the flow underground and undermine the walls.

sentences.zh: 益發戍甲卒十八萬酒泉、張掖北，置居延、休屠以衛酒泉。

translations.literal: He further raised garrison armored soldiers one hundred eighty thousand north of Jiuquan and Zhangye, established Juyan and Xiutu to guard Jiuquan.
translations.idiomatic: Another hundred eighty thousand armored men were posted north of Jiuquan and Zhangye, with Juyan and Xiutu garrisons shielding Jiuquan.

sentences.zh: 而發天下七科適，及載□給貳師，轉車人徒相連屬至敦煌。

translations.literal: And he raised the empire's seven categories of transport levy, and loaded [lacuna: □] to supply Ershi; transport carts and men followed one another in succession to Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: The seven classes liable for corvée were called up, convoys (one commodity name missing in the text) were loaded for Ershi, and wagon trains of laborers stretched all the way to Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 而拜習馬者二人為執驅馬校尉，備破宛擇取其善馬雲。

translations.literal: And he appointed two men skilled with horses as colonels of the led horse, preparing for when Dayuan was broken to select and take its fine horses, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Two expert horsemen were named colonels of the led horse, ready to pick the best stock once Ferghana fell.

sentences.zh: 於是貳師後復行，兵多，所至小國莫不迎，出食給軍。

translations.literal: Thereupon Ershi afterward again marched; the troops were many; wherever they arrived the small states without exception welcomed them and brought out food to supply the army.
translations.idiomatic: When Ershi marched again, his host was so large that every petty state along the way opened its granaries.

sentences.zh: 至輪台，輪台不下，攻數日，屠之。

translations.literal: Reaching Luntai, Luntai would not submit; they attacked several days and slaughtered it.
translations.idiomatic: At Luntai, which refused to yield, they stormed for days and put the town to the sword.

sentences.zh: 自此而西，平行至宛城，兵到者三萬。

translations.literal: From this westward, going level they reached Dayuan city; troops that arrived were thirty thousand.
translations.idiomatic: West of that point they marched level ground to the Ferghana capital with thirty thousand effectives.

sentences.zh: 宛兵迎擊漢兵，漢兵射敗之，宛兵走入保其城。

translations.literal: Dayuan troops met and struck Han troops; Han troops shot and defeated them; Dayuan troops fled in and defended their city.
translations.idiomatic: Ferghana sortied; Han archery broke them, and they bolted behind the walls.

sentences.zh: 貳師欲攻郁成城，恐留行而令宛益生詐，乃先至宛，決其水原，移之，則宛固已憂困。

translations.literal: Ershi wished to attack Yucheng city, but feared delaying the march and making Dayuan increase deceit the more; thereupon he first reached Dayuan, cut its water source, and diverted it, so that Dayuan was already anxious and distressed.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi thought of striking Yucheng first but feared delay would let Ferghana grow bolder; he drove straight to the capital, cut the aqueduct, and left the city parched and desperate.

sentences.zh: 圍其城，攻之四十餘日。

translations.literal: They surrounded its city and attacked it over forty days.
translations.idiomatic: They invested the walls for more than forty days.

sentences.zh: 其外城壞，虜宛貴人勇將煎靡。

translations.literal: Its outer wall broke; they captured Dayuan's great man, the brave general Jianmi.
translations.idiomatic: The outer ring fell; Han took the noble general Jianmi prisoner.

sentences.zh: 宛大恐，走入中城，相與謀曰：「漢所為攻宛，以王毋寡。」

translations.literal: Dayuan was greatly afraid, fled into the inner city, and plotted together, saying, "What Han does in attacking Dayuan is because of King Wugua."
translations.idiomatic: Panic sent them into the inner citadel, where nobles muttered, "Han is here for King Wugua."

sentences.zh: 宛貴人謀曰：「王毋寡匿善馬，殺漢使。

translations.literal: Dayuan's great men plotted, saying, "King Wugua hid fine horses and killed Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: The grandees said, "Wugua hid the horses and murdered the envoys.

sentences.zh: 今殺王而出善馬，漢兵宜解；

translations.literal: Now if we kill the king and bring out fine horses, Han troops ought to disperse;
translations.idiomatic: Kill him, hand over the studs, and Han should lift the siege;

sentences.zh: 即不，乃力戰而死，未晚也。」

translations.literal: if not, then we fight with full force and die—it is not yet late."
translations.idiomatic: if not, we fight to the death—there is still time."

sentences.zh: 宛貴人皆以為然，共殺王。

translations.literal: Dayuan's great men all deemed it right and together killed the king.
translations.idiomatic: They agreed and slew the king together.

sentences.zh: 持其頭，遣人使貳師，約曰：「漢無攻我，我盡出善馬，恣所取，而給漢軍食。

translations.literal: They held his head, sent men to Ershi, and covenanted, saying, "If Han does not attack us, we will bring out all fine horses, let you take as you will, and moreover supply Han army food.
translations.idiomatic: They sent his head to Ershi with terms: "Spare us, we will parade every good horse for your choice and feed your army."

sentences.zh: 即不聽我，我盡殺善馬，康居之救又且至。

translations.literal: If you do not heed us, we will kill all fine horses, and moreover Kangju's relief will soon arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Refuse, and we slaughter the bloodstock; Kangju's relief is already on the way."

sentences.zh: 至，我居內，康居居外，與漢軍戰。

translations.literal: When they arrive, we dwell within, Kangju dwells without, and we fight Han troops.
translations.idiomatic: When they come, we hold the inner city while Kangju strikes from without."

sentences.zh: 孰計之，何從？」

translations.literal: Weigh it carefully—which course to follow?"
translations.idiomatic: Weigh our choices—which way do you take?"

sentences.zh: 是時，康居候視漢兵尚盛，不敢進。

translations.literal: At this time Kangju spied and saw Han troops were still strong and did not dare advance.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju scouts saw how strong Han still was and hung back.

sentences.zh: 貳師聞宛城中新得漢人知穿井，而其內食尚多。

translations.literal: Ershi heard that within Dayuan city newly obtained Han men knew how to dig wells, and moreover food within was still abundant.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi learned that Han prisoners inside were sinking wells and that the citadel still held ample grain.

sentences.zh: 計以為來誅首惡者毋寡，毋寡頭已至，如此不許，則堅守，而康居候漢兵罷來救宛，破漢軍必矣。

translations.literal: He reckoned that they had come to execute the chief evil-doer Wugua; Wugua's head had already arrived; if in this case he did not consent, then they would firmly hold the defense, while Kangju waited for Han troops to tire and come save Dayuan—breaking Han troops was certain.
translations.idiomatic: Han had marched to punish Wugua; with that head delivered, to refuse the offer meant a prolonged siege while Kangju waited for Han exhaustion—and Han would surely be broken.

sentences.zh: 軍吏皆以為然，許宛之約。

translations.literal: The army officers all deemed it right and granted Dayuan's covenant.
translations.idiomatic: His officers agreed, and he accepted Ferghana's offer.

sentences.zh: 宛乃出其馬，令漢自擇之，而多出食食漢軍。

translations.literal: Dayuan thereupon brought out its horses, ordered Han to choose them itself, and moreover brought out much food to feed Han troops.
translations.idiomatic: Ferghana led out its horses for Han to pick and piled up rations for the army.

sentences.zh: 漢軍取其善馬數十匹，中馬以下牝牡三千餘匹，而立宛貴人之故時遇漢善者名昧蔡為宛王，與盟而罷兵，終不得入中城，罷而引歸。

translations.literal: Han troops took their fine horses several tens of head, and below middle-grade stallions and mares over three thousand head, and established the Dayuan great man who in the past had treated Han well, named Mocai, as king of Dayuan, swore covenant with him and halted troops; in the end they could not enter the inner city, halted and led the army back.
translations.idiomatic: Han took several dozen top mounts and more than three thousand lesser animals, enthroned the pro-Han noble Mocai, swore peace, and withdrew without ever entering the inner citadel.

sentences.zh: 初，貳師起孰煌西，為人多，道上國不能食，分為數軍，從南北道。

translations.literal: At first, when Ershi rose west of Dunhuang (the transmitted text uses a garbled form of the place name), because the men were many the states along the road could not feed them; he divided into several armies, following south and north routes.
translations.idiomatic: When Ershi first marched west from Dunhuang, his host was too large for the oases along the route to feed, so he split into several columns on northern and southern tracks.

sentences.zh: 校尉王申生、故鴻臚壺充國等千餘人別至郁成，城守不肯給食。

translations.literal: Colonel Wang Shensheng, former grand herald Hu Chongguo, and over a thousand others separately reached Yucheng; it guarded the walls and was unwilling to supply food.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Wang Shensheng and the former grand herald Hu Chongguo led a thousand men to Yucheng, which barred its gates and refused provisions.

sentences.zh: 申生去大軍二百里，負而輕之，攻郁成急。

translations.literal: Shensheng was two hundred li from the great army, relied on this and treated them lightly, and attacked Yucheng urgently.
translations.idiomatic: Two hundred li from the main army, Shensheng grew reckless and pressed the assault on Yucheng.

sentences.zh: 郁成窺知申生軍少，晨用三千人攻殺申生等，數人脫亡，走貳師。

translations.literal: Yucheng spied and knew Shensheng's troops were few; at dawn it used three thousand men to attack and kill Shensheng and the rest; several men escaped and fled to Ershi.
translations.idiomatic: Yucheng saw how small his force was and at dawn sent three thousand men to wipe it out; only a handful escaped to Ershi.

sentences.zh: 貳師令搜粟都尉上官桀往攻破郁成，郁成降。

translations.literal: Ershi ordered the search-grain commandant Shangguan Jie to go and break Yucheng; Yucheng submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi sent Search-Grain commandant Shangguan Jie, who stormed Yucheng into submission.

sentences.zh: 其王亡走康居，桀追至康居。

translations.literal: Its king fled and ran to Kangju; Jie pursued to Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: Its king fled to Kangju, and Jie followed him there.

sentences.zh: 康居聞漢已破宛，出郁成王與桀，桀令四騎士縛守詣大將軍。

translations.literal: Kangju heard Han had already broken Dayuan, brought out the king of Yucheng and gave him to Jie; Jie ordered four mounted knights to bind and guard him and convey him to the grand general.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that Han had taken Ferghana, Kangju handed over the king of Yucheng; Jie assigned four horsemen to bind him and escort him to the grand general.

sentences.zh: 四人相謂「郁成，漢所毒，今生將，卒失大事。」

translations.literal: The four said to one another, "Yucheng is what Han hates; now alive as a captive, in the end we lose the great affair."
translations.idiomatic: The four muttered, "Yucheng is Han's bitter enemy; delivering him alive will ruin everything."

sentences.zh: 欲殺，莫適先擊。

translations.literal: They wished to kill him, but none was appropriate to strike first.
translations.idiomatic: They meant to kill him, yet none would strike the first blow.

sentences.zh: 上□騎士趙弟拔劍擊斬郁成王。

translations.literal: The [lacuna] mounted knight Zhao Di drew his sword, struck, and beheaded the king of Yucheng.
translations.idiomatic: The cavalryman Zhao Di (one character missing before his title in the text) drew steel and cut down the king of Yucheng.

sentences.zh: 桀等遂追及大將軍。

translations.literal: Jie and the rest thereupon caught up with the grand general.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and his party then rejoined the grand general's column.

sentences.zh: 初，貳師後行，天子使使告烏孫大發兵擊宛。

translations.literal: At first, when Ershi marched later, the Son of Heaven sent envoys to inform Wusun to raise a great army and strike Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: During Ershi's second march the emperor sent word to Wusun to mobilize heavily against Ferghana.

sentences.zh: 烏孫發二千騎往，持兩端，不肯前。

translations.literal: Wusun raised two thousand cavalry to go, held both ends, and was unwilling to advance.
translations.idiomatic: Wusun sent two thousand horse but sat on the fence and would not close with the enemy.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍之東，諸所過小國聞宛破，皆使其子弟從入貢獻，見天子，因為質焉。

translations.literal: When the Ershi general went east, the various small states he passed heard Dayuan was broken and all sent their sons and younger brothers to follow in and present tribute, see the Son of Heaven, and thereby become hostages.
translations.idiomatic: On Ershi's return march every petty state along the road, hearing Ferghana had fallen, sent princes in his train to court with tribute and stayed as hostages.

sentences.zh: 軍還，入玉門者萬餘人，馬千餘匹。

translations.literal: The army returned; those who entered Jade Gate were over ten thousand men, horses over a thousand head.
translations.idiomatic: More than ten thousand men and a thousand horses crossed back through the Jade Gate.

sentences.zh: 後行，非乏食，戰死不甚多，而將吏貪，不愛卒，侵牟之，以此物故者眾。

translations.literal: On the later march it was not lack of food; battle deaths were not very many, but generals and officers were greedy, did not cherish the soldiers, and encroached on them; for this reason casualties from such causes were many.
translations.idiomatic: The second expedition did not starve and lost few in battle, yet greedy officers preyed on the ranks, and most deaths came from that abuse.

sentences.zh: 天子為萬里征伐，不錄其過，乃下詔曰：「匈奴為害久矣，今雖徙幕北，與旁國謀共要絕大月氏使，遮殺中郎將江、故雁門守攘。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven because of the ten-thousand-li campaign and conquest did not record their faults; thereupon he issued an edict saying, "The Xiongnu have been a harm long; now although they have moved north of the desert curtain, they plot with neighboring states jointly to intercept and cut off Greater Yuezhi envoys, intercepting and killing palace gentleman Jiang and former Yanmen guardian Rang.
translations.idiomatic: For a march of ten thousand li the emperor overlooked their faults and proclaimed: "The Xiongnu have long plagued the border; though driven north of the desert, they still league with neighbors to ambush Greater Yuezhi embassies and have cut down Palace Gentleman Jiang and former Yanmen warden Rang."

sentences.zh: 危須以西及大宛皆合約殺期門車令、中郎將朝及身毒國使，隔東西道。

translations.literal: West of Yanxu and including Dayuan all jointly in covenant killed Expectant Gates Che Ling, palace gentleman Chao, and the Shendu-state envoy, cutting off east-west roads.
translations.idiomatic: From Yanxu west to Ferghana they conspired to murder Expectant Gates Che Ling, Palace Gentleman Chao, and the Indian envoy, severing the road between east and west.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍廣利征討厥罪，伐勝大宛。

translations.literal: Ershi general Guangli campaigned and punished their crimes, attacked and overcame Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: General Ershi Li Guangli marched to punish those crimes and conquered Ferghana.

sentences.zh: 賴天之靈，從溯河山，涉流沙，通西海，山雪不積，士大夫徑度，獲王首虜，珍怪之物畢陳於闕。

translations.literal: Relying on heaven's numinous power, following upstream rivers and mountains, crossing shifting sands, opening to the western sea, mountain snow not accumulating, gentlemen officers went straight across, captured kings' heads and captives, and strange precious things were all displayed at the palace gate.
translations.idiomatic: By heaven's favor they traced rivers, crossed the Gobi, reached the western sea, marched snow-free heights, took enemy kings captive, and heaped rare tribute before the palace.

sentences.zh: 其封廣利為海西侯，食邑八千戶。」

translations.literal: Let Guangli be enfeoffed marquis of Haixi, with a fief of eight thousand households."
translations.idiomatic: Let Li Guangli be enfeoffed as Marquis of Haixi with eight thousand households.

sentences.zh: 又封斬郁成王者趙弟為新畤侯；

translations.literal: Moreover he enfeoffed Zhao Di, who had beheaded the king of Yucheng, as marquis of Xinzhai;
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Di, who had executed the king of Yucheng, was also enfeoffed as Marquis of Xinzhai;

sentences.zh: 軍正趙始成功最多，為光祿大夫；

translations.literal: the army rectifier Zhao Shi had the most merit in success and was made grand counsellor of the palace;
translations.idiomatic: Army Rectifier Zhao Shi, who had piled up the greatest merit, became grand counsellor of the palace;

sentences.zh: 上官桀敢深入，為少府；

translations.literal: Shangguan Jie dared to go deep in and was made privy treasurer;
translations.idiomatic: Shangguan Jie, commended for pressing deep into enemy country, was named privy treasurer;

sentences.zh: 李哆有計謀，為上黨太守。

translations.literal: Li Duo had stratagems and was made governor of Shangdang.
translations.idiomatic: Li Duo, valued for his counsel, was appointed governor of Shangdang.

sentences.zh: 軍官吏為九卿者三人，諸侯相、郡守、二千石百餘人，千石以下千餘人。

translations.literal: Of army officers and clerks, three became one of the nine ministers; over a hundred became marquisate chancellors, commandery governors, or holders of two-thousand-dan rank; over a thousand were at one-thousand-dan rank and below.
translations.idiomatic: Three officers rose to the nine senior ministries; more than a hundred became chancellors of nobles, commandery governors, or two-thousand-dan officials; over a thousand received ranks of a thousand dan or less.

sentences.zh: 奮行者官過其望，以適過行者皆黜其勞。

translations.literal: Those who strove to march had offices exceeding their hopes; those who marched with faults through banishment all had their merit dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: The eager were promoted beyond their dreams; those who had gone in disgrace saw their service struck from the rolls.

sentences.zh: 士卒賜直四萬錢。

translations.literal: Soldiers were granted cash worth forty thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: Common soldiers each received a bounty equivalent to forty thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 伐宛再反，凡四歲而得罷焉。

translations.literal: The attack on Dayuan went back and forth twice; in all four years and then it was able to be halted.
translations.idiomatic: The two Ferghana expeditions ran four years in all before the armies could stand down.

sentences.zh: 後十一歲，征和三年，貳師復將七萬騎出五原，擊匈奴，度郅居水。

translations.literal: After eleven years, in the third year of Zhenghe, Ershi again led seventy thousand cavalry out of Wuyuan, struck the Xiongnu, and crossed the Zhiju water.
translations.idiomatic: Eleven years later, in Zhenghe 3 (90 BCE), Ershi took seventy thousand horse out of Wuyuan against the Xiongnu and crossed the Zhiju River.

sentences.zh: 兵敗，降匈奴，為單于所殺。

translations.literal: The army was defeated; he surrendered to the Xiongnu and was killed by the chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: His army broke; he went over to the Xiongnu and the chanyu had him killed.

sentences.zh: 語在《匈奴傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the "Treatise on the Xiongnu."
translations.idiomatic: The account is given in the Xiongnu treatise.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：「《禹本紀》言河出崑崙，崑崙高二千五百里餘，日月所相避隱為光明也。

translations.literal: The eulogium says, "The Basic Annals of Yu states that the river issues from Kunlun; Kunlun is over two thousand five hundred li in height, and sun and moon each avoid and hide there to make brightness.
translations.idiomatic: The summation reads: "The Basic Annals of Yu places the river's source on Kunlun, claiming that peak rises more than two thousand five hundred li, so high that sun and moon pass behind it to shine."

sentences.zh: 自張騫使大夏之後，窮河原，惡睹所謂崑崙者乎？

translations.literal: From the time Zhang Qian was sent to Daxia afterward, tracing the river's source to the end—how can one see what is called Kunlun?
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Qian's mission to Daxia traced the source, what was left of that so-called Kunlun?

sentences.zh: 故言九州山川，《尚書》近之矣。

translations.literal: Therefore in speaking of the nine provinces' mountains and rivers, the Documents comes near to it.
translations.idiomatic: For the mountains and rivers of the nine provinces, the Classic of Documents comes far closer to the truth.

sentences.zh: 至《禹本紀》、《山經》所有，放哉！」

translations.literal: As for what the Basic Annals of Yu and the Classic of Mountains contain—how reckless!"
translations.idiomatic: As for the Basic Annals of Yu and the Classic of Mountains—sheer fancy!"
